content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 12, 2017

Robert G. Gottschalk
Counsel for SMCCTA
475 El Camino Real, Suite 300
Millbrae, CA 94030

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-264

Dear Mr. Gottschalk:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Pacifica Mayor Mike O'Neil,
Daly City Councilmember Mike Cuppano, Millbrae City Councilmember Wayne J. Lee, and South
San Francisco Director of Economic and Community Development Mike Lappen (collectively, the
"Traveling Public Officials") regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note that the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder
of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide
assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTIONS

1. Is the provision of roundtrip airfare by the San Mateo County China Trade Association
(SMCCTA), a nonprofit organization under Section 501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Code, to each
of the Traveling Public Officials to facilitate their attendance on the SMCCTA's trip to China, the
purpose of which was to encourage investment and trade between China and San Mateo County,
subject to the Act's $470 gift limit?

2. Are actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence provided by local Chinese
city governments to the Traveling Public Officials while the Officials were on the SMCCTA's trip
to China subject to the gift limit?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. Because the SMCCTA is a 501(c)(6) nonprofit organization, it is not eligible for the
exception to the gift limit for certain travel payments, advances, and reimbursements set forth in
Section 89506(a)(2), and therefore its provision of roundtrip airfare for each of the Traveling Public
Officials to attend the SMCCTA's trip to China was subject to the gift limit. Accordingly, each of

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-264
Page No. 2

the Traveling Public Officials should "pay down" the airfare within 30 days of its receipt in order to
avoid receipt of a gift in excess of the gift limit.

2. No. Local Chinese city governments are eligible for the gift-limit exception set forth in
Section 89506(a)(2) because they are "foreign governments" for purposes of that exception. Those
foreign governments' provision of actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence to the
Traveling Public Officials while they were in China was reasonably related to an issue of
international public policy. Therefore, Section 89506(a)(2)'s gift-limit exception applies, and those
foreign governments' provision of actual transportation and related lodging and substance to the
Traveling Public Officials while they were in China is not subject to the gift limit.²

FACTS

The SMCCTA is a 501(c)(6) nonprofit organization. Its objective is to assist in
communication between local governments and businesses in China and those in San Mateo
County. It acts to encourage Chinese businesses to locate or invest in businesses in the County, and
to assist local businesses which may wish to invest or locate in Southeastern China.

The SMCCTA invited the Traveling Public Officials to join local business persons for its
trip to China from November 4, 2017 through November 13, 2017.³ Other delegation members
include Roy Tan of New York Life Insurance, Paul Seto of Liberty Bank, and Baron Suen of Time
Advertising. The major objective of the trip to China is to encourage investment and trade between
China and San Mateo County.

The delegation visited Beijing, Shijiazhuang, Hangzhou, and Chengdu. In these cities, the
delegation met with local government officials, participated in the 2017 Overseas Chinese Elites
Summit for Innovation and High Tech Forum, visited the Chengdu New Economic Zone and
Entrepreneurs Forum, and toured the Hebei Luguan Optical Valley Science and Technology Zone.

The SMCCTA provided roundtrip airfares from San Francisco to China for the Traveling
Public Officials. All remaining expenses, including transportation, lodging, and subsistence while
on the trip were provided to the Traveling Public Officials by local Chinese city governments.

ANALYSIS

The Act's Gift Provisions:

Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as:

² We note, however, that any other gifts made by the local Chinese city governments to the Traveling Public
Officials other than the provision of "actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence" would be subject to the
gift limit unless another exception applies.

³ Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) authorizes the Commission to decline to provide formal written advice if the
requestor is seeking advice relating to past conduct. Because your request for advice was received on November 3,
2017, prior to the beginning of the SMCCTA's trip to China, and because it addresses potential gifts that may be "paid
down" pursuant to Section 82028(b)(2), we provide this advice prospectively in consideration of that rule.

File No. A-17-264
Page No. 3

"[A]ny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official status."

To reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by
public officials in three ways:

• Gift Limit of $470: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public
officials. (Section 89503.) The current limit is $470 from a single source in a calendar year.
(Regulation 18940.2.)

• Gift Reporting: So that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift of $50 or more
(or gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the reporting
period is disclosed on the official's statements of economic interests. (Section 87207;
Regulation 18940(d).)

• Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence the outcome of a
governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $470 or
more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date
the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).)

Gifts of Travel:

Under the Act, travel is presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public official. Thus,
absent an applicable exception, travel costs paid for by a third party for which the official does not
provide consideration of equal greater value are generally gifts subject to reporting and
regulation under the Act.

Section 89506:

Under some circumstances, however, although a travel payment, advance, or reimbursement
may be a reportable gift, the gift limit is not applicable. Section 89506(a)(2) sets forth an exception
to the Act's gift limits for certain travel payments, advances, or reimbursements, and provides in
pertinent part as follows:

"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to
a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if
either of the following applies:

***

File No. A-17-264
Page No. 4

"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the United States who substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code."

Thus, travel payments, advances, or reimbursements to a public official for actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence that are reasonably related to an issue of international public policy are not subject to the Act's $470 gift limit if the travel is provided by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization or a foreign government.

SMCCTA's Travel Payments

The SMCCTA provided roundtrip airfares to the Traveling Public Officials that took part in its trip to China. The SMCCTA's provision of the roundtrip airfares was reasonably related to issues of international public policy because the purpose of its trip to China was to encourage investment and trade between China and San Mateo County. However, the SMCCTA is a nonprofit organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(6), rather than Section 501(c)(3), of the Internal Revenue Code. Therefore, the exception to the gift limit set forth in Section 89506(a)(2) is not applicable to the SMCCTA's provision of roundtrip airfares to the Traveling Public Officials to attend the SMCCTA's trip to China, and those roundtrip airfares are subject to the gift limit.

Section 8202B(b)(2) provides that the term "gift" does not include payments which are not used, returned to the donor within 30 days of receipt, or delivered to a 501(c)(3) nonprofit entity without being claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes. Accordingly, each of the Traveling Public Officials may "pay down" the roundtrip airfare from SMCCTA within 30 days of its receipt pursuant to Section 8202B(b)(2) to avoid receipt of a gift in excess of the gift limit.

Local Chinese City Governments' Travel Payments

Local Chinese city governments covered all remaining expenses of the Traveling Public Officials that took part in the SMCCTA's trip to China, including transportation, lodging, and meals while on the trip. Those governments' provision of "actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence" was also reasonably related to issues of international public policy because the purpose of the SMCCTA trip to China was to encourage investment and trade between China and San Mateo County.

We have previously determined that a local government of a foreign country is a "foreign government" for purposes of Section 89506(a)(2)'s gift limit exception. (Overmeyer Advice Letter, No. I-14-118; Reyes et al Advice Letter, No. A-14-071; Peterson Advice Letter, No. A-97-554.)

Therefore, the local Chinese city governments at issue are foreign governments for purposes of that exception.

File No. A-17-264
Page No. 5

Because the local Chinese governments' provision of actual transportation and related
lodging and subsistence is reasonably related to issues of international public policy, and because
those governments are foreign governments, Section 89506(a)(2)'s gift limit exception applies, and
the transportation, lodging, and subsistence provided by those foreign governments to the Traveling
Public Officials while in China are not subject to the Act's gift limits. However, we note that any
gifts made by those foreign governments to the Traveling Public Officials other than "actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence" would be subject to the Act's gift limits unless
another exception applies.

Conflict of Interest Disqualification?

As discussed above, a public official who receives gifts of $470 or more may have a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).) If gifts of $470 or
more were "provided to, received by, or promised to" the official within the 12 months preceding
the governmental decision at issue, the official would have a financial interest in the source of the
gifts under Section 87103(e). If the official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning
of Section 87100, the official must recuse himself or herself from taking part in that decision in
accordance with Section 87105 or Regulation 18707.

The facts presented provide no indication of a forthcoming governmental decision before
any of the Traveling Public Officials' respective cities that may have a disqualifying financial effect
on those Officials' potential financial interests in SMCCTA or the local Chinese city governments
as sources of the travel payments at issue. However, if such a decision arises, you should seek
further advice providing all relevant facts.4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

4 We note that certain nonprofits that regularly organize and hosts travel for elected officials may also be
required to disclose the names of certain donors to the Commission under Section 89506(f). However, because as you
have not asked for assistance regarding the Act's reporting rules as applicable to SMCCTA, we do not address Section
89506(f) further.
